metadata.summary: Configure WireGuard VPN server with split-tunnel dev machine, full-tunnel mobile, and site-to-site home gateway. Cryptokey routing for access control.

content.title: WireGuard VPN
content.summary: Configure WireGuard as a VPN server on a Linux VPS with multiple peer types: split-tunnel dev machine (VPN subnet only), full-tunnel mobile (all traffic), and site-to-site gateway (VPS to home LAN). Uses cryptokey routing — each peer's AllowedIPs list determines which packets are routed to it.

why.p: WireGuard is ~4,000 lines vs OpenVPN's 100,000+, runs as a kernel module (built-in since Linux 5.6), and achieves near-wire speed. For solo developers it eliminates exposing database ports publicly — services bind to 127.0.0.1 on the VPS and are accessible only through the VPN tunnel.

list.item: Accessing internal VPS services (PostgreSQL, Redis, RabbitMQ) securely without exposing ports to the internet
Creating a site-to-site tunnel between a VPS and home network/Raspberry Pi
Routing all mobile traffic through the VPS for privacy on public Wi-Fi
Restricting SSH access to VPN subnet only (after VPN is confirmed working)
When you only need SSH access — an SSH tunnel (`ssh -L`) is simpler and requires no server-side setup
When your provider already offers a managed VPN or private networking between servers — use that instead
As a replacement for UFW — WireGuard controls which hosts can connect; UFW controls which ports are exposed. Both are needed

section.name: cryptokey-routing
section.p: When a packet arrives on a WireGuard interface, the source IP is checked against every peer's AllowedIPs. If it matches, the packet is accepted and decrypted with that peer's key. If it does not match any peer's AllowedIPs, the packet is silently dropped.

rule.statement: AllowedIPs serves two purposes: ingress filter (which source IPs to accept) and egress routing table (which destination IPs to send to this peer).
rule.rationale: This dual role is the core of WireGuard's simplicity — no separate routing table entries needed, no certificate authority, just keys and IP ranges.

example.code:
# Server sees packets from 10.0.0.2 → trusts them if signed by laptop's key # Server sees packets for 10.0.0.2 → encrypts and sends to laptop
Peer (laptop): AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32 Peer (RPi + LAN): AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.3/32, 192.168.1.0/24

section.name: key-types
section.p: Three types of keys: private key (generated with `wg genkey`, never leave device, chmod 600), public key (derived from private with `wg pubkey`, share with peers), preshared key (optional symmetric key for post-quantum resistance, generated with `wg genpsk`).

rule.statement: Private key — generated with `wg genkey`. Never leave the device. chmod 600.
rule.rationale: Private keys must be protected from unauthorized access. chmod 600 ensures only the owner can read.

rule.statement: Public key — derived from private key with `wg pubkey`. Share with all peers who need to connect to you.
rule.rationale: Public keys are safe to share publicly; they are cryptographically derived from the private key.

rule.statement: Preshared key — optional symmetric key shared between two specific peers. Adds a post-quantum resistance layer. Generated with `wg genpsk`.
rule.rationale: Preshared keys provide defense-in-depth against potential future quantum computers that might break ECDH.

section.name: network-architecture
section.p: VPS acts as the hub. All peers connect to it. Peer-to-peer traffic routes through the VPS unless direct routing is configured.

example.code: VPS wg0: 10.0.0.1/24 (hub) Peer: dev laptop 10.0.0.2/32 (split tunnel) Peer: home RPi 10.0.0.3/32 + 192.168.1.0/24 (site-to-site) Peer: phone 10.0.0.4/32 (full tunnel)

section.name: full-tunnel-vs-split-tunnel
section.p: The only difference is AllowedIPs on the client config. Full tunnel: `AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0` — all traffic routes through VPN. Split tunnel: `AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.0/24` — only VPN subnet uses tunnel, internet goes direct.

section.name: server-interface-section
section.p: Server Interface section specifies PrivateKey, Address /24, ListenPort, and PostUp NAT rules. IP forwarding required on server.

example.code:
[Interface] PrivateKey = <server-private-key> Address = 10.0.0.1/24 ListenPort = 51820 SaveConfig = false
# NAT — replace eth0 with actual interface (ip route | grep default) PostUp = iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE PostDown = iptables -D FORWARD -i wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE

rule.statement: Use Address = x.x.x.1/24 on the server (not /32) so the wg0 interface owns the entire /24 subnet.
rule.rationale: A /32 on the server means the server only has its own IP. With /24, the kernel knows all 10.0.0.x addresses are reachable via wg0 and routes them correctly.

section.name: peer-section
section.p: Each peer gets a Peer section with PublicKey, PresharedKey, AllowedIPs, and PersistentKeepalive for NAT traversal.

example.code:
# Laptop — split tunnel [Peer] PublicKey = <laptop-pubkey> PresharedKey = <laptop-preshared-key> AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.2/32
# Home RPi — site-to-site with LAN access [Peer] PublicKey = <rpi-pubkey> PresharedKey = <rpi-preshared-key> AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.3/32, 192.168.1.0/24 PersistentKeepalive = 25
# Phone — full tunnel [Peer] PublicKey = <phone-pubkey> PresharedKey = <phone-preshared-key> AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.4/32

rule.statement: Set PersistentKeepalive = 25 for peers behind NAT (home RPi, mobile).
rule.rationale: NAT tables expire idle UDP sessions. Without keepalive, a peer behind NAT will lose its ability to receive packets after ~30 seconds of silence.

example.code:
# /etc/sysctl.d/99-wireguard.conf net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1 net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding = 1
# Apply immediately sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-wireguard.conf

section.name: site-to-site-routing
section.p: To reach the home LAN (192.168.1.0/24) from any VPN peer: add the LAN subnet to the RPi peer's AllowedIPs on the server, and enable forwarding + NAT on the RPi.

example.code:
# Server: RPi peer entry [Peer] PublicKey = <rpi-pubkey> AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.3/32, 192.168.1.0/24
# RPi wg0.conf: forward traffic to/from home LAN [Interface] PostUp = iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o eth0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -A FORWARD -i eth0 -o wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE PostDown = iptables -D FORWARD -i wg0 -o eth0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -D FORWARD -i eth0 -o wg0 -j ACCEPT; iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -o eth0 -j MASQUERADE

section.name: dev-machine-split-tunnel
section.p: Goal: reach VPS internal services (PostgreSQL, Redis) without routing internet traffic through VPN. AllowedIPs includes only VPN subnet.

example.code:
# /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf on dev machine [Interface] PrivateKey = <dev-private-key> Address = 10.0.0.2/32
[Peer] PublicKey = <server-pubkey> PresharedKey = <preshared-key> Endpoint = SERVER_PUBLIC_IP:51820 AllowedIPs = 10.0.0.0/24 PersistentKeepalive = 25
example.note: Internet traffic continues via normal gateway. Only 10.0.0.x traffic uses VPN.

section.name: mobile-full-tunnel
section.p: Route all mobile traffic through VPS for privacy on public Wi-Fi.

example.code:
[Interface] PrivateKey = <phone-private-key> Address = 10.0.0.4/32 DNS = 1.1.1.1
[Peer] PublicKey = <server-pubkey> PresharedKey = <preshared-key> Endpoint = SERVER_PUBLIC_IP:51820 AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0, ::/0 PersistentKeepalive = 25
# Generate mobile config, then create QR code for import sudo cat /etc/wireguard/clients/phone.conf | qrencode -t ansiutf8

section.name: restrict-ssh-to-vpn-subnet
section.p: After confirming VPN works, restrict SSH access to VPN IPs. This eliminates the SSH attack surface for external scanners.

example.code:
# /etc/ssh/sshd_config AllowUsers nero@10.0.0.0/24
# Or UFW rule (after removing the 0.0.0.0 SSH rule) sudo ufw delete allow 22022/tcp sudo ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/24 to any port 22022 proto tcp comment 'SSH via VPN' sudo ufw reload
example.note: Test VPN SSH works before applying. Keep a rescue console session open.

example.code:
# Show all peers and last handshake time sudo wg show
# Check handshake age (stale = no handshake in 3+ min) sudo wg show wg0 latest-handshakes
# Data transferred per peer sudo wg show wg0 transfer
# Verify IP forwarding cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward # should be 1

template.purpose: Server config with multiple peer types (laptop, RPi+LAN, phone)
Mobile client config (scan as QR code)
IP forwarding settings for WireGuard server

script.purpose: Generate private, public, preshared keys for a new peer
Add peer to running interface, generate client config and QR code
Show peer handshake age, data transfer, active/stale status
